Diagnosis:
Fetal magnetic resonance imaging was performed at 31 weeks 2 days gestation. The cerebral cortex shows appropriate sulcation and opercularization for this gestational stage, matching the expected anatomical landmarks. The bilateral lateral ventricles are symmetrical and measure within normal limits, with an atrial width of 6.8 mm on the right and 7.1 mm on the left. The corpus callosum is fully formed and visualized in its entirety, showing normal thickness. Posterior fossa structures, including the cerebellar vermis and brainstem, are completely unremarkable.

Impression: Unremarkable fetal MRI at 31 weeks gestation. Fetal brain development and biometry are normal and appropriate for gestational age, with no structural or intracranial abnormalities identified.

Cardiac MRI demonstrates normal cardiac chambers and intact myocardial function. The left ventricular end-diastolic volume is 74 mL/m2 and the left ventricular ejection fraction is preserved at 64% with no regional wall motion abnormalities. The right ventricle is also normal in size and function, with an ejection fraction of 58%. No significant valvular regurgitation or stenosis is seen, and there is no evidence of myocardial edema or delayed enhancement to suggest myocarditis or prior infarct.

Impression: Normal cardiac MRI with preserved biventricular systolic function, normal chamber volumes, and no evidence of myocardial scar, fibrosis, or acute inflammation. This provides reassurance against underlying structural cardiomyopathy.

Brain MRI utilizing multiplanar, multisequence protocols with and without intravenous contrast. There is no evidence of intra-axial or extra-axial mass, acute cerebral infarction, or intracranial hemorrhage. The ventricular system, sulci, and cisterns are within normal limits for patient age, with no signs of hydrocephalus. The major intracranial arterial flow voids are well-preserved, and there are no areas of abnormal enhancement.

Impression: Unremarkable MRI of the brain. No structural abnormalities, ischemic changes, or pathological enhancement seen to explain the patient's symptoms of chronic localized migraine.

Cardiac MRI was performed on a 31-year-old postpartum female presenting with progressive dyspnea and lower extremity edema. Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) is severely depressed at 32%, with global hypokinesis of the left ventricular myocardium. Left ventricular end-diastolic volume (LVEDV) is indexed at 112 mL/m2, representing moderate cavity dilation. Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) imaging shows no evidence of focal myocardial scar, fibrosis, or subendocardial infarction, typical for non-ischemic pathology.

Impression: Severely reduced left ventricular systolic function with global hypokinesis and moderate chamber dilation. In the clinical context of recent delivery, the findings are highly suggestive of peripartum cardiomyopathy. No myocardial fibrosis is identified on late gadolinium enhancement, which carries a favorable prognosis for potential recovery.

Brain MRI was performed in this 34-year-old postpartum patient presenting with acute onset of severe headache, altered mental status, and seizures. There are bilateral, symmetric areas of increased T2 and FLAIR signal intensity primarily involving the subcortical white matter of the parieto-occipital lobes, without associated restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC sequences. No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage or main dural venous sinus thrombosis is identified. The ventricles and basal cisterns remain within normal limits.

Impression: Bilateral, symmetric parieto-occipital subcortical signal abnormalities, highly characteristic of Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES), particularly in the clinical context of severe postpartum preeclampsia. Clinical correlation and close blood pressure management are recommended; findings typically resolve with control of the underlying etiology.

Fetal MRI performed at 22 weeks 4 days gestation for further evaluation of suspected central nervous system anomalies. There is complete absence of the corpus callosum. The lateral ventricles are dilated, with a classic tear-drop appearance (colpocephaly) where the posterior horns measure 13.5 mm on the left and 12.8 mm on the right. Radial arrangement of the gyri is noted on sagittal projections, converging towards the third ventricle, which is dorsally displaced. Cerebellar hemispheres, brainstem, and sulcation patterns are otherwise normal in size and morphology for gestational age.

Impression: Complete agenesis of the corpus callosum with associated colpocephaly and mild bilateral ventriculomegaly. Genetic counseling and pediatric neurology consultation are recommended.
